name: River Oceanus
shortDescription: The vast, primordial river encircling the world in Greek mythology, personified as a Titan.
description: In Greek mythology, Oceanus was the great, earth-encircling river, a powerful and primordial force. He was both a geographical feature and a Titan god, the eldest son of Uranus (Heaven) and Gaia (Earth). Oceanus represented the body of water that the Greeks believed surrounded the flat, disc-shaped Earth. From Oceanus flowed all other rivers, springs, and seas. He was seen as the source of all fresh water, and his presence was fundamental to the cosmos. While he predates the Olympian gods, Oceanus remained a respected figure, often depicted as a wise and benevolent elder. He is generally considered to have receded into the background of Greek myth as geographical knowledge increased, and the Greeks realized the Mediterranean Sea was not encircled by a single river. Oceanus’ wife was Tethys, a Titaness, and together they were the parents of numerous Oceanids (sea nymphs) and the Potamoi (river gods).
subtitle: The world-encircling river and Titan god.
characteristics: Father of Oceanids and river gods
significance: Represents the primordial waters and the boundary of the world.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: Oceanus was the eldest son of Uranus (Heaven) and Gaia (Earth), making him a Titan of the first generation. His wife was his sister, Tethys, and together they produced thousands of offspring, including the Oceanids, who were nymphs of springs, rivers, and seas, and the Potamoi, the gods of rivers. In Homer's *Iliad*, Oceanus is referenced as the source of all rivers and even the sea. He is depicted as a powerful but generally passive figure, not actively involved in the conflicts of the Olympian gods. During the Titanomachy, the war between the Titans and the Olympians, Oceanus sided with neither side. This neutrality allowed him to retain his position and respect even after the Olympians emerged victorious.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: Oceanus played a crucial role in the Greek understanding of the cosmos. He represented the boundary of the world, the limit of human exploration and knowledge. As the source of all fresh water, he was essential for life and agriculture. While he wasn't actively worshipped in temples like the Olympian gods, he was respected as a fundamental force of nature. Sailors and seafarers would acknowledge him, and his daughters, the Oceanids, were often invoked for safe passage. Oceanus’ significance diminished somewhat as Greek geographical knowledge expanded, and the concept of a single river encircling the world became untenable. However, he remained a powerful symbol of the primordial waters and the vastness of the unknown.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: Oceanus symbolizes the primordial waters from which all life emerged. He represents the boundary between the known and the unknown, the civilized world and the uncharted territories beyond. His neutrality during the Titanomachy can be interpreted as a symbol of cosmic balance and the enduring power of nature, unaffected by the conflicts of gods and mortals. The Oceanids, his daughters, represent the diverse and life-giving aspects of water, from gentle springs to powerful rivers. They embody the beauty and fertility of the natural world. Oceanus can be seen as a representation of the subconscious, the deep and mysterious realm from which creativity and inspiration flow.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Oceanus' influence can be seen in literature, art, and cartography throughout history. Ancient maps often depicted the world as surrounded by a great river, reflecting the Greek concept of Oceanus. He appears in numerous works of classical literature, including Homer's *Iliad* and Hesiod's *Theogony*. In modern times, Oceanus continues to be a source of inspiration for artists and writers. His name is used in various scientific contexts, particularly in oceanography. The concept of a world-encircling river has also resonated in fantasy literature and art, where it often symbolizes the boundary between different realms or the edge of the known world. Oceanus also appears in modern pop culture, for example in the video game *Hades*.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: Provides early descriptions of Oceanus as the source of all rivers and the sea.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: Details the genealogy of Oceanus and his role as a Titan.

associations.name: Water
associations.significance: Oceanus is the embodiment of primordial waters and the source of all rivers and seas.

associations.name: Boundary
associations.significance: Oceanus represents the edge of the world and the limit of human knowledge.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Jormungandr
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: A giant serpent encircling Midgard (the world), similar to Oceanus' encircling of the Earth.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Apsu
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Represents the primordial fresh water ocean that existed before the creation of the world.

companions.name: Tethys
companions.description: Titan goddess and wife of Oceanus, mother of the Oceanids and river gods.

companions.name: The Oceanids
companions.description: Three thousand daughters of Oceanus and Tethys, they are nymphs of springs, rivers, and seas.

cultural.modernLegacy: Oceanus remains a symbol of the primordial waters, the vastness of the ocean, and the boundaries of the known world, influencing art, literature, and scientific nomenclature.
